title: System Monitoring — Host Instrumentation Coverage vs Inventory

compliance.assurance_rationale: NIST 800-53 SI-4 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST 800-53 SI-4 (System monitoring) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.14.36: System Monitoring — Host Instrumentation Coverage vs Inventory.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.14.36' running on _internal splunkd connection metrics, lookup cmdb_hosts.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

app: Splunk Enterprise Deployment Server / Splunk Cloud Monitoring Console
spl:
index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=Metrics group=tcp, connections earliest=-4h
| stats latest(kb) as kb, dc(source) as peers by hostname
| rename hostname as host
| inputlookup append=t cmdb_hosts.csv
| stats values(in_scope) as in_scope, max(peers) as reporting by host
| where in_scope="true" AND isnull(reporting)
description: Compares active forwarders to CMDB inventory for SI-4 system monitoring coverage.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NIST 800-53 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes _internal and sourcetypes splunkd. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Replace internal metric approach with `| metadata type=hosts` nightly if simpler; (2) reconcile host naming; (3) alert missing Tier0; (4) document agent deployment standard (CM-7). Indexes required: `index=_internal`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=splunkd`; Lookups: ``cmdb_hosts.csv`.
visualization: Table (missing monitoring), Single value (coverage %), Choropleth (by site from lookup). Key fields: `host`, `hostname`, `in_scope`, `kb`, `peers`, `reporting`.
knownFalsePositives: Security or system signals can spike during maintenance, vendor tests, and lab cutovers — check scope, asset criticality, and the scan schedule before you call it a failure.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Deployment Server / Splunk Cloud Monitoring Console.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `_internal` `splunkd` connection metrics, lookup `cmdb_hosts.csv`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Replace internal metric approach with `| metadata type=hosts` nightly if simpler; (2) reconcile host naming; (3) alert missing Tier0; (4) document agent deployment standard (CM-7).

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=Metrics group=tcp, connections earliest=-4h
| stats latest(kb) as kb, dc(source) as peers by hostname
| rename hostname as host
| inputlookup append=t cmdb_hosts.csv
| stats values(in_scope) as in_scope, max(peers) as reporting by host
| where in_scope="true" AND isnull(reporting)
```

Understanding this SPL

**System Monitoring — Host Instrumentation Coverage vs Inventory (SI-4)** — Compares active forwarders to CMDB inventory for SI-4 system monitoring coverage.

Documented **Data sources**: `_internal` `splunkd` connection metrics, lookup `cmdb_hosts.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Deployment Server / Splunk Cloud Monitoring Console. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: _internal; **sourcetype**: splunkd. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=_internal, sourcetype=splunkd, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by hostname** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Renames fields with `rename` for clarity or joins.
• Loads rows via `inputlookup` (KV store or CSV lookup) for enrichment or reporting.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by host** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Filters the current rows with `where in_scope="true" AND isnull(reporting)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare a sample of the same events in the source system (domain controller, cloud tenant, or device) the security team would open first. Spot-check a few source events, reconcile counts, and verify field extractions, thresholds, and index permissions for the people who need this evidence.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (missing monitoring), Single value (coverage %), Choropleth (by site from lookup)
grandmaExplanation: We compare active forwarders to CMDB inventory for SI-4 system monitoring coverage. That helps federal and agency security reviews match what the systems actually do day to day.
